title: 5 3 Nonhomogeneous second order ODEs
body:
5.3 NON-HOMOGENEOUS SECOND ORDER ODES


The non-homogeneous second order ODE with constant coefficients

                                    ay 00 + by 0 + cy = r(t)

is solved by first solving the homogeneous problem

                                     ay 00 + by 0 + cy = 0

to obtain the homogenous solution yh (t). We then find the particular solution yp (t) by
using the method of undetermined coefficients or the method of variation of
parameters. The full solution is then y(t) = yh (t) + yp (t).

To find the long term steady state solution, consider the behaviour as t → ∞



We now deal with the constant coefficient second order ODEs with some simple functions
appearing on the RHS. This makes the ODE non-homogeneous. We begin by finding the
homogeneous solution yh (t) by using the methods of the previous lecture.

We then look for a particular solution yp (t). There are two methods that we can try:

   • The method of undetermined coefficients. With this method, we form a trial
     solution (basically an educated guess) that involves some unknown coefficients. We
     then solve for the coefficients by substituting our trial solution into the ODE.

   • The method of variation of parameters. If the method of undetermined coef-
     ficients fails, we can try this method. It is a much more technical process, however,
     and it involves integration (ugh!).

Once we have yh (t) and yp (t), the full solution is given by

                                    y(t) = yh (t) + yp (t)

Before we look at methods for finding yp (t), let us first ask: why do we need to include
the homogeneous solution yh (t) in the answer?

Proof:




                                              1
                The Method of Undetermined Coefficients

Example 1 Solve y 00 + 5y 0 + 6y = −6t + 25. Describe the long term steady state solution
to the differential equation.




           F y = Ae−2t + Be−3t − t + 5 Steady State Solution is y = −t + 5 F

                                           2
 The crucial question is of course how do we know what to guess for yp ? A rule of thumb
is to select the most general function (or combination of functions) that could be related
to the RHS and its derivatives. The following table gives some RHS’s, their derivatives,
and the corresponding guesses for yp .


    RHS           First derivatives        Second derivatives           Choice of yP

     3e4x                e4x                        e4x                     Ae4x

    x3 − 7                x2                         x              Ax3 + Bx2 + Cx + D

  3 sin(4x)            cos(4x)                    sin(4x)           A cos(4x) + B sin(4x)

 5e7x cos(2x) e7x cos(2x), e7x sin(2x) e7x cos(2x), e7x sin(2x) e7x (A cos(2x) + B sin(2x))

    9x2 e3x          x2 e3x , xe3x           x2 e3x , xe3x , e3x     e3x (A + Bx + Cx2 )


Example 2 Solve the initial value problem

                                 y 00 + y = 55e2x + 3x2 + 14

where y(0) = 20 and y 0 (0) = 28.




                                             3
                                       F y = cos(x) + 6 sin(x) + 11e2x + 3x2 + 8 F


General comments on the Method of Undetermined Coefficients:

  • A list of trial solutions for yh is printed on page 22 of the additional course notes.
    The undetermined coefficients are then obtained by substituting them into the non-
    homogeneous differential equation, matching coefficients, and solving the resulting
    set of simultaneous equations.

  • As a general rule, chose the most general solution that could satisfy the differential
    equation. Thus, if the RHS is a polynomial function like x2 −7 then the trial solution
    should be the most general polynomial of the same order: ax2 + bx + c. Likewise,
    if the RHS is a trigonometric function like sin(5x), the trial solution is the most
    general trigonometric solution with the same frequency: A cos(5x) + B sin(5x).

  • If the RHS is a sum or product of different function types (e.g. trigonometric + poly-
    nomial, polynomial × exponential) the trial solution should be the corresponding
    sum or product of trial solutions.

  • If a trial solution yp = f (x) is already a solution of the homogeneous problem, we
    form a new solution by multiplying by x: yp = xf (x). If the new solution is also
    a solution of the homogeneous problem (e.g. if the auxiliary equation has a double
    root) then we multiply by x again: yp = x2 f (x). Thus, it is very important to check
    that your trial solution does not contain any solutions of the homogeneous problem!


                                           4
                  The Method of Variation of Parameters

If the method of undetermined coefficients fails to produce a particular solution yp it is
possible that technique of variation of parameters will still work.



                             Variation of Parameters
Suppose that the second order differential equation

                               y 00 + p(x)y 0 + q(x)y = f (x)

has homogeneous solution yh = Ay1 (x) + By2 (x). Then a particular solution is given by
                                  Z                          Z
                                     y2 (x)f (x)               y1 (x)f (x)
               yP (x) = −y1 (x)                  dx + y2 (x)               dx
                                        W (x)                     W (x)
                                    !
                    y1 (x) y2 (x)
where W (x) = det                     .
                    y10 (x) y20 (x)


A full proof of this formula is found in the additional course notes.


Example 3 Use Variation of Parameters to solve y 00 + y = sec(x).




                                             5
                        F y = A cos(x) + B sin(x) + cos(x) ln(| cos(x)|) + x sin(x) F
1
    You can now do Q85a, Q85b, and Q88
